concepts.title: Venn Diagrams
concepts.conceptBody:
Use Of Vein Diagrams 

1. $\left( A\cap B \right)$

Interpretation: A occurs as well as B occurs or Both A and B occur simultaneously.

Example: 

If sample space is,

$\text{S}$ $~\equiv \left\{ 1,2,3,4,5,6 \right\}$

$\text{A}$ $~\equiv \left\{ 1,3,5 \right\}$

$\text{B}$ $~\equiv \left\{ 3,6 \right\}$

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)~=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}$

So from example, $\text{n}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)=\left\{ 3 \right\}=1$ because only 3 is common in both sets

n(S) = 6

$\therefore $ $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)=\frac{1}{6}$

2. $\left( A\cup B \right)$

Interpretation: Atleast one of A and B occur or either A or B or both occur.

Note: Here case of both can also be there in term of set, it is UNION of A & B.

 

Shaded Area represents the region required.

Formula: $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)$ 

why $-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)$ ?

This is because we have counted the region $R$ (which represent $A\cap B$ ) twice in $P\left( A \right)$ and $\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)$. $\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)$ includes the area ' $\text{R}$ ' (i.e. $\text{A}\cap \text{B}$ ) and $\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)$ also includes the same. So we have to subtract it once. How to calculate it then?

Let us take the sample example as taken earlier

S $~\equiv \left\{ 1,2,3,4,5,6 \right\}$

A $~\equiv \left\{ 1,3,5 \right\}$

B $~\equiv \left\{ 3,6 \right\}$

$\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)=\frac{3}{6}=\frac{1}{2}\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)$ $~=\frac{2}{6}=\frac{1}{3}$

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)$ $~=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}=\frac{1}{6}$

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B} \right)$ $~=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{6}$

$~=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{6}$

$~=\frac{4}{6}=\frac{2}{3}$

(a) Complementary Events: 

 

i.e. non-occurrence of $\text{A}$ (the shaded area in the diagram) So if $\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)$ is the probability of occurrence of $\text{A}$ then

$\text{P}\left( {\bar{A}} \right)=1-\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)$

as the entire space (in diagram entire rectangle has probability of occurrence as 1.

(b) Exhaustive Events 

A set of events is said to be exhaustive of atleast one of them happens when experiment takes place.

$\text{OR}$, the sample space is completely filled up by events (as shown in diagram)

Condition: 

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B}\cup C \right)=1$

(c) Mutually Exclusive Events: 

$\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ are two mutually exclusive events, if happening of A rules out the happening of $\text{B}$ or vice versa i.e. they cannot occur simultaneously in terms of set disjoint of $\text{A}\And \text{ B}$ is null set i.e., $\text{A}\cap \text{B}=\phi$

 

Condition: 

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)$

In general:

$\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}}\cup {{\text{A}}_{2}}\ldots .{{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}} \right)=\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)+\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)+\ldots .\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}} \right)$

where ${{A}_{1}},{{A}_{2}},{{A}_{3}}\ldots {{A}_{n}}$ are $n$ mutually exclusively events from diagram the area where $\text{A}~\And ~\text{ B}$ do not occur simultaneously is the answer.

3. Only A 

Interpretation: This means that only one of the events occur at a time.

Let us take the case of only A to understand it

represents: $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \bar{B} \right)$

Logic : We want the region of only $\text{A}$ and it we see the vein diagram there is a region $\text{A}\cap \text{B}$, i.e. some part of $\text{B}$ is also there hence $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \bar{B} \right)$, area of $\text{A}$ not containing (i.e. $\bar{B})\text{B}$.

formula: $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \bar{B} \right) ~=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)$

$~=\left\{ \text{region }\left( 1+2 \right) \right\}-\left\{ \text{ region }2 \right\}$

4. Exactly one of A and B 

Interpretation: It means that either only A occur or only B occurs, but no occurrence of event containing both A & B.

 

Shaded region represents it

Note: No $\left( A\cap B \right)$ region included.

$\text{ formula }=\text{P}\left( \text{ only }\text{ A} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{ only }\text{ B} \right)$

$=\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \bar{B} \right)+\text{P}\left( \bar{A}\cap \text{B} \right)$

$=\left\{ \text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right) \right\}+\left\{ \text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right) \right\}$

$=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)-2\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)$ 
as $(\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)=(\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)$

= $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B} \right)-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)$

Also you can just simply derive the formula from vein diagram.

$\text{ shaded }\text{ region }=\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B} \right)-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)$

example, extending the earlier one

we know $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B} \right)=\frac{2}{3}$

and $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)=\frac{1}{6}$

$\therefore \text{ Required }\text{ Answer }=\frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{6}$

$=\frac{3}{6}=\frac{1}{2}$

5. None of A and B 

Interpretation: It means that neither A nor B occurs.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{ formula } & =\text{P}\left( \bar{A}\cap \bar{B} \right) \\ {} & =\text{P}\left( \bar{A}\cup \bar{B} \right) \\ {} & =1-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B} \right) \\ \end{array}$
(you can see from above diagram)

Note: Now since we have covered so many types let us discuss another formula for $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B} \right)$.

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B} \right) =\text{P}\left( \text{ only }\text{ A} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{ only }\text{ B} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{ Both }\text{ A }\text{ and }\text{ B} \right)$

$=\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \bar{B} \right)+\text{P}\left( \bar{A}\cap \text{B} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)$

1. $P\left( A\cap B\cap C \right)$ 

 

Interpretation: Occurrence of all the events A, B and C simultaneously.

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{S} \right)}$

2. $P\left( A\cup B\cup C \right)$ 

 

Interpretation: either A or B or C occurs or all of them can occur

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B}\cup \text{C} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{C} \right)$

$-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{C} \right)-\text{P}\left( \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)$

Why add region 1?

We have subtracted the region 13 times in $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right),\text{P}\left( \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)$ and $\text{P}\left( \text{C}\cap \text{A} \right)$, hence we need to add it to that $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)$, i.e. event where all can occur can be counted.

 

1. Only A 

As done in case of 2 event $\text{P}($ only $\text{A})=\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \bar{B}\cap \bar{C} \right)$ can say

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{P}\left( \text{ only }\text{ A} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{C} \right) \\ +\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right) \\ \end{array}$

Note: Again we have added the part $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)$ logic is same as explained in previous part. Think it for yourself.

2. Exactly 1 event occurs: 

this is equal to

 

$\text{P}($ only $\text{A})+\text{P}($only $\text{B})+\text{P}($ only $\text{C})$

$=\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \bar{B}\cap \bar{C} \right)+\text{P}\left( \bar{A}\cap \text{B}\cap \bar{C} \right)+\text{P}\left( \bar{A}\cap \bar{B}\cap \text{C} \right)$

put the values using the above result to get

$=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{C} \right)-2\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)-2\text{P}\left( \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)$

$-2\text{P}\left( \text{C}\cap \text{A} \right)+3\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)$

3. Exactly 2 events occur : 

 

Interpretation: 

either A and B occur or B and C occur or C and A occur.

Note: Region 1 is not included here.

So from vein diagram, deriving the formula

$\text{P}($ exactly 2$)=\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{C}\cap \text{A} \right)$

$-3\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)$

again notice the subtraction of $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)$, as all terms contain it, so have to subtract it.

 

4. Atleast 2 events occur: 

Interpretation: This means that minimum of 2 events should occur. So 3 events occuring simultaneously is perfectly fine in this case

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{P}\left( \text{ atleast }2 \right)= & \text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{C}\cap \text{A} \right) \\ {} & -2\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B}\cap \text{C} \right). \\ \end{array}$
